problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem B (discrete): design land-use policies/management strategies for Kenya's Maasai Mara reserve that balance wildlife/natural-resource conservation against the economic interests of surrounding residents, minimize negative human-animal interactions, rank candidate strategies, predict long-term outcomes, transfer the approach to another reserve, and write a non-technical report to the Kenya Tourism and Wildlife Committee. The team discretized the reserve into a 36-cell grid and assigned each cell one of four functional zones (wildlife sanctuary, agricultural, hunting, tourism), then optimized, evaluated, and forecast.
models_used: Model I — Grid-discretization + 0-1 (binary) integer programming with dual objectives: 36 equal-area cells (50 km^2 each), decision variables X_j/Y_j/Z_j/W_j in {0,1} with mutual-exclusion constraint X_j+Y_j+Z_j+W_j=1; objectives max ecological benefit (sum X_j*V_jx) and max economic benefit (sum of Y/Z/W times their unit values); solved in LINGO yielding 3 non-dominated scenarios

Ecological benefit index — normalized composite: min-max normalized vegetation cover plus a rarity-weighted animal index (lion=1, elephant=0.8, giraffe=0.4, zebra=0.5, antelope=-0.3), combined as V_x = B1*A_x + B2*P_x; threshold rules on V_x gate which zone types are admissible per cell (>0.5 sanctuary only, 0.4-0.5 add tourism, 0.2-0.4 add hunting, <0.2 allow agriculture)

Economic benefit model — tourism revenue Tr = Nt*Als*Ads*Lts; per-unit-area values for agriculture/hunting/tourism derived from Kenya national output scaled by area; government/resident profit split r1=0.2, r2=0.8 with a residents'-income-floor constraint (>= $2082/yr per-capita Kenya income)

Model II — Minimum-interaction model on a graph: reserve zones as nodes in graph G(V,E), inter-zone interaction weights (Table 7) on edges, Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm used to MAXIMIZE separation distance between sanctuaries and human-activity zones (least interaction); interaction intensity P = sum d_ij*w_ij + sum b_n*w_n
Economic impact evaluation model — total economic value per scenario E_i = Q_ix*V_X + Q_iy*V_y + Q_iz*V_z + Q_iw*V_w; used to break ties among the 3 scenarios (Scenario 2 best at $154,948.974M)

Model III — Long-term trend forecasting: quadratic non-linear regression of tourism revenue vs tourist count (y = 2979420 - 37718x + 122.9x^2, R^2=0.926 vs linear R^2=0.912); growth-rate assumption (visitors +1% vs prior +0.3%) projected 3 years forward
Hypothesis test — independent two-sample t-test on Kenya tourist numbers pre- vs post-COVID (t=4.354, p=0.049<0.05) to demonstrate force-majeure sensitivity of the forecast
Model migration — re-parameterized constraints (income floor $65,627 Wyoming, visitor cap 10,904/day) to transfer the same pipeline to Yellowstone National Park
key_techniques: Spatial discretization: turning a continuous reserve into a 36-cell grid so a continuous land-use problem becomes a tractable 0-1 assignment problem

Operationalizing vague problem language: explicitly defining 'resources', 'lost opportunities', 'people attracted to the preserve', and 'negative interactions' (split into People->Wildlife and Wildlife->People categories) before modeling
Multi-objective handling via non-dominated scenario set: dual-goal LP intentionally yields no single optimum but 3 candidate scenarios, deferring the choice to the Problem-2 evaluation stage
Two-stage decision: generate candidates (Model I) then rank them (Dijkstra interaction score + economic value) so Problems 1 and 2 are coupled rather than independent
Creative reuse of Dijkstra: rather than minimizing travel distance, they maximize sanctuary-to-human distance as a proxy for minimizing negative interaction
Index construction with explicit normalization (min-max) and rarity weights to put heterogeneous ecological quantities on a common [0,1] scale
Constraint engineering tying a computed quantity (V_x ecological value) to feasibility of categorical choices via threshold-gated constraints
Model-fit comparison (linear vs quadratic R^2) to justify the chosen regression form
Robustness check: perturbing factor weights by <=0.1 across four 'special cases' (food crisis, natural disaster, policy change, financial crisis) and confirming Scenario 2 stays best with R^2>0.8
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on completeness and narrative coherence rather than on mathematical depth. It answers every sub-question (4 problems including the letter), defines ambiguous terms up front, and threads a single grid-based framework through optimization, evaluation, forecasting, and transfer so the work reads as one connected story rather than four disconnected exercises. The two-stage 'generate candidates then rank them' design is genuinely elegant: it lets the dual-objective LP punt on the trade-off and resolves it with a second, independent criterion (graph-distance interaction + economic value). The creative repurposing of Dijkstra to maximize separation distance is the kind of memorable, problem-specific idea judges reward. It is strong on the MCM scoring rubric staples: clear assumptions with justifications, a notation table, heavy and well-captioned visualization, a sensitivity analysis showing the winning scenario is robust, a model-migration section (Yellowstone) proving generality, and a polished non-technical letter. The team also handled real data (collected, cleaned, visualized, cited sources) and validated forecasts with a hypothesis test, signaling rigor.
section_highlights: Definitions section (3.2) is a standout — pre-empting ambiguity by pinning down 'negative interactions' into two directional categories before any math. The Assumptions section pairs every assumption with a justification (the MCM-expected format). The flowchart (Figure 1) in 'Our work' gives judges a one-glance map of the entire solution. Problem 1 cleanly separates decision variables (Table 3), constraints (mutual exclusion, sanctuary-dominance, tourist-cap, residents'-income floor), and objective functions. The virtuous-cycle feedback diagram (Figure 9) communicates the long-term policy dynamics intuitively. The sensitivity section uses concrete numbers (weights perturbed <=0.1, R^2 reported per case) rather than hand-waving. The non-technical letter (Problem 4) is appropriately audience-tuned: vivid opening, plain recommendations, no formulas. Tools/data are disclosed in the appendix (LINGO 17, MATLAB R2021b, Python 3.8) with a raw data table.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Data fabrication risk: the problem provided no data, and several key numbers (per-unit-area economic values, animal rarity weights like antelope=-0.3, inter-zone interaction weights in Table 7) appear chosen/assumed with thin sourcing — a negative weight for a species in an 'ecological benefit' index is conceptually questionable

Internal inconsistency in numbers: the 36-grid times 50 km^2 implies 1800 km^2 (matches Table 11), but the appendix data table units and the multi-thousand-million economic figures are hard to reconcile and look loosely calibrated; sensitivity-analysis economic values (~1.1-1.3M) differ by an order of magnitude from the main-text values (130k-155k million) with no reconciliation

Dijkstra is arguably overkill/misapplied: with zones on a grid and uniform edge length d, 'shortest path between adjacent zone types' is largely a grid-distance computation; the algorithm adds sophistication-signaling more than necessity, and the interaction-intensity equation (eq 17) mixes a shortest-path term with an unexplained linear-regression term b_n*w_n whose coefficients are never fit

Forecast rests on an unjustified assumption (visitors +1% vs historical +0.3%) with no mechanism linking the rezoning to that specific uplift; the quadratic regression is fit on only ~10 points and extrapolated
Notation sloppiness: symbols drift (V_x vs J_x, garbled subscripts), and the notation table admits 'some variables are not listed'
Threshold gating on V_x (0.2/0.4/0.5 cutoffs) is arbitrary with no calibration or sensitivity on the thresholds themselves
Model migration to Yellowstone reuses '36 grids' framing and the same 40-people-per-tourist-area constant despite Yellowstone being ~5x larger, weakening the transfer's credibility
English-language and typo issues throughout (likely a non-native team) that could cost polish points
reusable_patterns: Grid discretization to convert a continuous spatial/resource-allocation problem into a 0-1 integer-programming assignment — broadly reusable for any 'where to put what' MCM problem
Mutual-exclusion constraint (sum of binaries per cell = 1) plus dominance constraints (one class must outnumber others) as a clean template for categorical land/resource assignment

Two-stage 'generate a non-dominated candidate set with a multi-objective optimizer, then rank candidates with a separate evaluation model' — a robust pattern for coupling an open-ended design question with a 'which is best' follow-up
Build a composite normalized index (min-max + category weights) to fuse heterogeneous quantities into one comparable score, then expose the weights to a sensitivity sweep
Repurpose a classic graph algorithm (Dijkstra) for a non-obvious objective (maximize separation as a proxy for minimizing conflict) — a template for earning 'creativity' credit
Define ambiguous problem terms explicitly in a Definitions section before modeling, and split a fuzzy concept (interactions) into directional sub-categories
Validate a time-series forecast with a hypothesis test (t-test) to surface force-majeure/structural-break risk (COVID) rather than presenting the forecast as certain
Demonstrate generality with an explicit model-migration section that re-parameterizes constraints for a second case study (Yellowstone) using a feasibility-comparison table
Sensitivity analysis by small bounded weight perturbations (<=0.1) across a small set of named scenario shocks, reporting that the winner and goodness-of-fit are preserved
Pair every assumption with a one-line justification, include a notation table, a master flowchart, and an audience-appropriate non-technical letter — the structural scaffolding judges expect
